title: Modularity Emerges from Action-Functional Constraints in Marine Metabolic Networks: A Biology-Scale Validation of the Network-Weighted Action Principle
comment: 49 pp, 10 figs. Companion papers: Frasch 2026a (J Physiol, DOI:10.1113/JP290762), 2026b (arXiv:2603.16951), 2026c (arXiv:2604.24805). Code: https://github.com/martinfrasch/tara-modularity
summary: Biological systems operate under simultaneous energetic and informational constraints, yet direct evidence that such constraints shape real metabolic networks is limited. The Network-Weighted Action Principle predicts that networks under these constraints should organize toward high modularity. We tested this prediction in marine microbiome metabolic networks reconstructed from Tara Oceans metagenomes using two complementary approaches. Composite metrics of protein-deployment efficiency and functional-repertoire complexity (n=10) failed under causal-inference diagnostics, with apparent structure dominated by shared-component bias. In contrast, network modularity (n=7) was high (Q ~ 0.987), but this value was shown to arise from sparsity alone. The biologically meaningful signal is the excess over null models: modularity exceeded configuration-model, label-permutation, and bipartite-incidence nulls by Delta Q ~ 0.15-0.40 (p < 0.001), with the largest effect under the bipartite-incidence control. Fine-grained communities recovered by the network partition are not arbitrary: 25% recur across samples, and the most consistent modules map to known functional units, including enzyme subunits, biosynthetic sequences, and transporter complexes. Together, these results show that modularity excess - rather than absolute modularity - is the appropriate signature of biological organization, and that such excess is consistent with cost-minimization principles operating at the scale of natural metabolic networks.

AI.tldr: Marine microbiome metabolic networks show high absolute modularity (~0.987) driven by sparsity, but the biologically meaningful signal is modularity excess over null models (ΔQ ~ 0.15-0.40, p<0.001), with 25% of communities recurring across samples and mapping to known functional units—consistent with cost-minimization principles.
AI.motivation: To test whether real metabolic networks are shaped by simultaneous energetic and informational constraints (as predicted by the Network-Weighted Action Principle), specifically whether they organize toward high modularity.
AI.method: Reconstructed metabolic networks from Tara Oceans metagenomes; tested composite metrics of protein-deployment efficiency and functional-repertoire complexity (n=10, failed causal-inference diagnostics); measured network modularity (n=7) and compared against configuration-model, label-permutation, and bipartite-incidence null models.
AI.result: Absolute modularity was high (~0.987) but driven by sparsity; biologically meaningful signal is modularity excess over nulls (ΔQ ~ 0.15-0.40, p<0.001). 25% of fine-grained communities recur across samples, mapping to enzyme subunits, biosynthetic sequences, and transporter complexes.
AI.conclusion: Modularity excess—not absolute modularity—is the appropriate signature of biological organization in metabolic networks, and such excess is consistent with cost-minimization principles operating at the scale of natural metabolic networks.

title: Enhancing Cryo-EM Density Map Segmentation in Phenix for Improved Atomic Model Building
summary: We introduce PhenixCraft, a fully automated pipeline for building atomic models from cryo-EM density maps. By integrating AlphaFold predictions, we enhance the map-segmentation step in Phenix during model building, addressing challenges posed by noise and artifacts that traditionally hinder this step. Our results demonstrate PhenixCraft's superior performance in TM-scores and sequence accuracy, significantly improving upon the limitations and inefficiencies of traditional model building using Phenix.

AI.tldr: PhenixCraft is an automated pipeline that integrates AlphaFold predictions with Phenix to improve atomic model building from cryo-EM density maps, achieving better TM-scores and sequence accuracy than traditional methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of traditional cryo-EM model building in Phenix, particularly issues with noise and artifacts that hinder accurate map segmentation during atomic model construction.
AI.method: A fully automated pipeline that integrates AlphaFold protein structure predictions into the map-segmentation step of Phenix for cryo-EM density map analysis.
AI.result: Superior performance demonstrated through improved TM-scores and sequence accuracy compared to traditional Phenix model building approaches.
AI.conclusion: PhenixCraft effectively addresses noise and artifact challenges in cryo-EM model building, offering a more accurate and efficient automated solution.

title: Chapter 2: Geometry of the Fitness Surface and Trajectory Dynamics of Replicator Systems
summary: We study the geometry of the mean fitness surface of replicator systems and its relationship to evolutionary trajectory dynamics. Using the symmetric--antisymmetric decomposition of the fitness landscape matrix, we derive an explicit formula for the rate of change of mean fitness and establish necessary conditions for its monotonicity along trajectories. In general, replicator trajectories do not reach the maximum of the fitness surface, even in the presence of a unique asymptotically stable equilibrium. We characterise, in terms of the symmetric and antisymmetric parts of the fitness matrix, the precise conditions under which an equilibrium coincides with a local extremum of the fitness surface. Circulant matrices are identified as a natural and nontrivial class satisfying these conditions. We establish a two-way connection between fitness surface maxima and evolutionarily stable states: evolutionary stability implies a local fitness maximum, and the converse holds under the identified structural conditions. When the unique asymptotically stable equilibrium is a local maximum, it is evolutionarily stable and realises the global maximum of the fitness surface; an unstable equilibrium forces the global maximum to the boundary of the simplex. The framework is extended to general Lotka--Volterra systems, where an analogue of mean fitness is shown to share the same extremal properties. Results are illustrated through six examples spanning autocatalytic and hypercyclic replication, a parametric family exhibiting Andronov--Hopf bifurcation and heteroclinic cycles, and the Eigen quasispecies model.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the geometry of the mean fitness surface in replicator systems, deriving conditions for fitness monotonicity and equilibrium-fitness extremum coincidence using symmetric-antisymmetric matrix decomposition, with connections to evolutionarily stable states and extensions to Lotka-Volterra systems.
AI.motivation: To understand the geometric relationship between the mean fitness surface and evolutionary trajectory dynamics in replicator systems, particularly whether trajectories reach fitness maxima and under what conditions equilibria correspond to fitness extrema.
AI.method: Symmetric-antisymmetric decomposition of the fitness landscape matrix; derivation of explicit formula for rate of change of mean fitness; analysis of circulant matrices as a special class; connections to evolutionary stability theory; extension to general Lotka-Volterra systems.
AI.result: Replicator trajectories generally do not reach fitness surface maxima even with stable equilibria. Precise conditions (via symmetric/antisymmetric parts) are identified for equilibrium-fitness extremum coincidence. Circulant matrices naturally satisfy these conditions. Evolutionary stability implies local fitness maximum, with converse under identified conditions. Global maximum achieved when stable equilibrium is a local maximum; otherwise forced to boundary.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a geometric understanding of fitness surfaces in evolutionary dynamics, with applications to autocatalytic replication, hypercycles, Andronov-Hopf bifurcations, heteroclinic cycles, and the Eigen quasispecies model.

title: The Origin of Life in the Light of Evolution
comment: This article is currently under review
summary: The origin of life is often framed primarily as a chemical problem, yet life's defining feature is evolution. Advances in geochemistry, prebiotic chemistry, and molecular biology have produced diverse scenarios for the emergence of genomes, metabolism, and cellular compartments on the early Earth, but most of these models lack a population-genetics framework. Here, we argue that origin-of-life research must expand from asking simply how life began to exploring how it evolved from pre-biological systems. Synthesizing evidence from comparative genomics, phylogenetics, biochemistry, and geoscience, we emphasize that the last universal common ancestor (LUCA) was already a complex, ecologically adapted population far removed from the starting point of life, implying a deep pre-LUCA evolutionary history. We highlight how population genetics, ecology, and synthetic biology can constrain origin-of-life scenarios by making explicit the roles of selection, drift, mutation, horizontal gene transfer, parasites, and compartmentalization in shaping early communities. Finally, we outline an evolutionary research agenda spanning protometabolic and autocatalytic networks, protocells, the emergence of translation, and the transition to DNA genomes, in which qualitative models can now be buttressed and formalized by evolution-driven hypotheses subject to testing using theory and laboratory experiments, including those with synthetic cells.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that origin-of-life research must shift focus from purely chemical questions to evolutionary frameworks, emphasizing that LUCA was already complex, and advocating for population genetics, ecology, and synthetic biology approaches to understand pre-biological evolution.
AI.motivation: Current origin-of-life models lack a population-genetics framework, focusing too narrowly on chemistry while ignoring that life's defining feature is evolution. The authors aim to expand the field to include how life evolved from pre-biological systems.
AI.method: The authors synthesize evidence from comparative genomics, phylogenetics, biochemistry, and geoscience, and highlight how population genetics, ecology, and synthetic biology can constrain origin-of-life scenarios by modeling selection, drift, mutation, horizontal gene transfer, parasites, and compartmentalization.
AI.result: LUCA was already a complex, ecologically adapted population, implying a deep pre-LUCA evolutionary history. The authors identify key areas (protometabolic networks, protocells, translation emergence, DNA genome transition) where evolution-driven hypotheses can be experimentally tested.
AI.conclusion: Origin-of-life research should adopt an evolutionary research agenda, formalizing qualitative models with evolution-driven hypotheses testable via theory and laboratory experiments, including synthetic cells.

title: Planar morphometry via functional shape data analysis and quasi-conformal mappings
summary: The study of shapes is one of the most fundamental problems in life sciences. Although numerous methods have been developed for the morphometry of planar biological shapes over the past several decades, most of them focus solely on either the outer silhouettes or the interior features of the shapes without capturing the coupling between them. Moreover, many existing shape mapping techniques are limited to establishing correspondence between planar structures without further allowing for the quantitative analysis or modelling of shape changes. In this work, we introduce FDA-QC, a novel planar morphometry method that combines functional shape data analysis (FDA) techniques and quasi-conformal (QC) mappings, taking both the boundary and interior of the planar shapes into consideration. Specifically, closed planar curves are represented by their square-root velocity functions and registered by elastic matching in the function space. The induced boundary correspondence is then extended to the entire planar domains by a quasi-conformal map, optionally with landmark constraints. Moreover, the proposed FDA-QC method can naturally lead to a unified framework for shape morphing and shape variation quantification. We apply the FDA-QC method to various leaf and insect wing datasets, and the experimental results show that the proposed combined approach captures morphological variation more effectively than purely boundary-based or interior-based descriptions. Altogether, our work paves a new way for understanding the growth and form of planar biological shapes.

AI.tldr: FDA-QC combines functional data analysis (elastic matching of boundaries) with quasi-conformal mappings (interior deformation) to jointly analyze planar biological shapes like leaves and insect wings, enabling both shape morphing and quantification of morphological variation.
AI.motivation: Existing planar morphometry methods focus on either outer silhouettes or interior features separately, failing to capture the coupling between them, and many shape mapping techniques cannot quantitatively analyze or model shape changes.
AI.method: Closed planar curves are represented by square-root velocity functions and registered via elastic matching; boundary correspondence is extended to the entire domain using quasi-conformal maps (optionally with landmark constraints), yielding a unified framework for shape morphing and variation quantification.
AI.result: Applied to leaf and insect wing datasets, FDA-QC captures morphological variation more effectively than purely boundary-based or interior-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The combined boundary-interior approach paves a new way for understanding the growth and form of planar biological shapes.

title: MP2D: Constrained Monte Carlo Tree-Guided Diffusion for Multi-Objective Protein Sequence Design
comment: 16 pages, 4 figures, 7 tables, accepted by the 35th International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence
summary: Designing functional protein sequences that satisfy multiple desired properties is a core research focus of protein engineering. Prior methods struggle with inability or inefficiency when dealing with numerous, often conflicting, properties. We propose Multi-Property Protein Diffusion (MP2D), a unified framework for multi-objective protein sequence optimization that integrates conditional discrete diffusion with constrained MCTS and global iterative refinement. MP2D formulates diffusion denoising as a constrained sequential decision-making process and employs MCTS to explore diverse denoising trajectories guided by Pareto-based rewards. A global iterative refinement strategy further enables repeated remasking and re-optimization of candidate sequences, while a dynamic Pareto constraint prevents candidate bloat and maintains balanced trade-offs across objectives. We evaluate MP2D on two challenging multi-objective protein design tasks: antimicrobial peptide and protein binder optimization, involving four to five conflicting properties. Experimental results demonstrate that MP2D consistently outperforms existing multi-objective baselines, achieving robust and balanced improvements across all objectives without retraining generative models. These results highlight MP2D as a practical and scalable solution for multi-objective functional protein design.

AI.tldr: MP2D is a unified framework combining conditional discrete diffusion, constrained MCTS, and global iterative refinement to efficiently optimize protein sequences across multiple conflicting objectives, outperforming existing baselines in antimicrobial peptide and protein binder design.
AI.motivation: Existing methods are unable or inefficient at handling multiple, often conflicting, properties in protein sequence optimization.
AI.method: Integrates conditional discrete diffusion with constrained Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) and global iterative refinement; formulates denoising as constrained sequential decision-making; uses Pareto-based rewards and dynamic Pareto constraints to prevent candidate bloat.
AI.result: Outperforms existing multi-objective baselines on antimicrobial peptide and protein binder optimization tasks (involving 4-5 conflicting properties), achieving robust and balanced improvements without retraining generative models.
AI.conclusion: MP2D is a practical and scalable solution for multi-objective functional protein design.

title: Decoding Alignment without Encoding Alignment: A critique of similarity analysis in neuroscience
summary: Decoding approaches are widely used in neuroscience and machine learning to compare stimulus representations across neural systems, such as different brain regions, organisms, and deep learning models. Popular methods include decoding (perceptual) manifolds and alignment metrics such as Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) and Dynamic Similarity Analysis (DSA), where similarity in decoding representations is interpreted as evidence for similar computation. This paper demonstrates a fundamental weakness behind this approach: it is misleading to assume that representational geometry is representative of a neuronal population as a whole, when such representations may actually be shaped by a very small subset of neurons. We show that the complementary encoding paradigm addresses this issue directly: it characterizes how neurons are organized globally in terms of their responses to a set of data, providing insight into how the decoding representation is implemented by neurons within a population. We demonstrate across experiments in biological systems and deep learning models that (i) surprisingly, similar decoding behavior and high representational alignment can arise from small, non-representative subpopulations of neurons; and critically, (ii) alignment metrics are insensitive to encoding manifold topology (how function is distributed across neurons), despite this being a key signature of differentiation across biological systems. A controlled MNIST experiment provides causal evidence: decoding metrics remain unchanged even when encoding topology is causally manipulated via the training loss. Overall, similarity in decoding behavior, as measured by classic alignment metrics, does not imply similarity in function or computation, motivating the use of encoding manifolds as a complementary tool for comparing neural systems.

AI.tldr: Decoding-based representational similarity metrics (e.g., RSA, DSA) are misleading because they can be driven by small, non-representative neural subpopulations and are insensitive to encoding manifold topology, whereas the encoding paradigm reveals how neural population function is actually organized.
AI.motivation: Current alignment metrics (decoding manifolds, RSA, DSA) assume similar representational geometry implies similar computation across neural systems, but this assumption may be flawed if representations are shaped by a minority of neurons.
AI.method: The authors compare decoding and encoding paradigms across biological neural systems and deep learning models, using controlled MNIST experiments to causally manipulate encoding topology while measuring decoding metrics.
AI.result: (i) Similar decoding behavior and high alignment can arise from small, non-representative subpopulations; (ii) alignment metrics are insensitive to encoding manifold topology; (iii) causally altering encoding topology via training loss leaves decoding metrics unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Decoding similarity does not imply functional similarity, and encoding manifolds should be used as a complementary tool for comparing neural systems.

title: Towards a unified framework for multiple stable states in ecological systems
summary: Multiple stable states - the coexistence of two or more distinct ecological configurations under identical environmental conditions - have attracted sustained interest in ecology, yet the field still lacks a unified framework connecting ecological mechanisms to dynamical models. Here, we review empirical and theoretical approaches to multiple stable states, synthesising perspectives on stability, tipping, hysteresis, and transient dynamics, and contextualise these within a common mathematical framework. Drawing on examples of well-known ecosystem models, we highlight the central and necessary role of positive feedback loops and identify other common, unifying features of ecological systems that exhibit multiple stable states. We further discuss the relationship between stable and transient dynamics, the roles of spatial and temporal scales in feedback identification, and the implications for ecological restoration and management. We conclude with open questions and challenges for the field, including extending multistability theory to persistent-transient frameworks and harnessing emerging data-collection technologies to sharpen empirical inference.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews and synthesizes empirical and theoretical research on multiple stable states in ecology, proposing a unified mathematical framework centered on positive feedback loops, discussing transient dynamics, spatial-temporal scales, and implications for restoration and management.
AI.motivation: The field lacks a unified framework connecting ecological mechanisms to dynamical models for multiple stable states, despite sustained interest in the topic.
AI.method: Review and synthesis of empirical and theoretical approaches, using a common mathematical framework and well-known ecosystem models to identify unifying features.
AI.result: Positive feedback loops are identified as central and necessary for multiple stable states; other common unifying features across ecosystems are highlighted.
AI.conclusion: The field should extend multistability theory to persistent-transient frameworks and leverage new data-collection technologies to improve empirical inference.

title: Higher-order interactions in ecology can be hidden in plain sight
summary: Higher-order interactions are increasingly recognized as a key component of ecological dynamics. However, we show that higher-order Lotka-Volterra dynamics can, in some scenarios, be accurately reproduced by effective pairwise models fitted to the same abundance time series. Consequently, higher-order interactions cannot, in general, be inferred from time-series data alone. We further identify a fundamental problem of mechanistic identifiability, whereby distinct interaction mechanisms generate nearly indistinguishable dynamics, potentially leading to accurate yet misleading ecological interpretations. Our results highlight the need to complement time-series data with additional ecological information to infer interaction structure reliably.

AI.tldr: Higher-order Lotka-Volterra dynamics can sometimes be reproduced by pairwise models, making it impossible to reliably infer higher-order interactions from time-series data alone due to mechanistic identifiability issues.
AI.motivation: Higher-order interactions are recognized as key in ecology, but it remains unclear whether they can be reliably inferred from time-series data.
AI.method: The authors compare higher-order Lotka-Volterra dynamics with effective pairwise models fitted to the same abundance time-series data.
AI.result: Higher-order dynamics can be accurately reproduced by pairwise models, and distinct interaction mechanisms can generate nearly indistinguishable dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Time-series data alone is insufficient; additional ecological information is needed to reliably infer interaction structure.

title: A multi-scale information geometry reveals the structure of mutual information in neural populations
summary: Understanding how neural population responses represent sensory information is a central problem in systems neuroscience. One approach is to define a representational geometry on stimulus space in which distances reflect how reliably stimuli can be distinguished from neural activity. However, different constructions of these distances can lead to qualitatively different conclusions about the neural code. Here, we show that a unique Riemannian representational geometry emerges from first principles governing how distances contract as stimulus resolution is lost through coarse-graining. This results in a multi-scale extension of the Fisher information metric, capturing encoding structure from fine stimulus details to coarse global distinctions. The resulting geometry is exactly related to the mutual information encoded by the population: well encoded stimulus directions - those contributing more to mutual information - are expanded, whereas poorly encoded directions are contracted. The metric tensor can be estimated using diffusion models, making the framework practical for large neural populations and high-dimensional stimuli. Applied to visual cortical responses to natural images, the eigenvectors of the metric tensor identify stimulus variations that contribute most to information transmission, yielding interpretable features that are robust to modelling choices. Together, these results provide a principled, information-theoretic framework for characterising neural population codes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a principled Riemannian geometry framework for neural population codes, where distances contract as stimulus resolution is lost through coarse-graining, producing a multi-scale extension of the Fisher information metric that is directly linked to mutual information and can be estimated via diffusion models.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches to define representational geometry in neural population responses can lead to qualitatively different conclusions about the neural code, lacking a principled foundation.
AI.method: The authors derive a unique Riemannian representational geometry from first principles based on how distances contract under coarse-graining of stimulus resolution, yielding a multi-scale extension of the Fisher information metric that relates to mutual information, and estimate the metric tensor using diffusion models.
AI.result: The metric tensor's eigenvectors identify stimulus variations contributing most to information transmission, yielding interpretable features robust to modelling choices, demonstrated on visual cortical responses to natural images.
AI.conclusion: This provides a principled, information-theoretic framework for characterizing neural population codes.

title: Beyond Object-Level Alignment: Do Brains and DNNs Preserve the Same Transformations?
summary: Brain-DNN alignment is usually assessed through stimulus-level correspondence or stimulus-set geometry. Inspired by category theory, we operationalize a different question: do brain and model preserve the same candidate transformations among stimuli? We formalize this as approximate naturality: if a proxy-defined stimulus change is propagated through the brain side and then translated to the model side, the result should match translating first and then propagating, so that the naturality square approximately commutes. We quantify deviations from commutativity by a Naturality Violation Score (NVS) normalized to a permutation null, shifting alignment from per-stimulus sameness to preservation of structure under an explicitly chosen comparison map. As a proof of concept, a controlled five-factor synthetic setting shows that NVS separates complementary alignment failures that aggregate object- and geometry-level scalars cannot resolve. Applied to fMRI responses from the GOD dataset (5 subjects), 3 vision DNNs, and 3 World-Model proxy embeddings, the axis-resolved analysis reveals a hierarchy crossover: semantic axes align most strongly toward HVC and deeper DNN layers (NVS^animacy = 0.39 vs 0.52 for the next-best axis and 1.0 for the permutation-null baseline), whereas low- and mid-level visual axes align toward earlier visual cortex and shallower layers. Supporting analyses (a 15-axis appendix atlas, dissociation tests against RSA/CKA and encoding/decoding accuracy, and a W-less anchor-ablation control) confirm that the alignment is selective over candidate morphism families rather than uniform. NVS thereby turns brain-DNN comparison into a test of jointly preserved candidate transformations, relative to an explicit proxy space and permutation null, and opens a path to richer proxy spaces and controlled world-side transformations.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Naturality Violation Score (NVS) based on category theory to assess brain-DNN alignment by checking whether stimulus transformations are preserved consistently across brain and model, revealing hierarchy-specific alignment (e.g., semantic axes align with deep DNN layers, visual axes with early layers).
AI.motivation: Existing brain-DNN alignment metrics (stimulus-level correspondence, geometry-level measures like RSA/CKA) cannot distinguish whether transformations among stimuli are jointly preserved. The authors aim to formalize alignment as whether the brain and model preserve the same candidate transformations (morphisms) among stimuli.
AI.method: The authors formalize approximate naturality from category theory: a transformation propagated through brain then translated should match translating first then propagating through model. Deviations are quantified by NVS (normalized to a permutation null). Tested on synthetic data (5 factors), fMRI from GOD dataset (5 subjects), 3 vision DNNs, and 3 World-Model proxies.
AI.result: Synthetic data shows NVS captures complementary failures missed by object/geometry-level metrics. In fMRI data, semantic axes align best with HVC and deeper DNN layers (NVS^animacy=0.39 vs next-best 0.52), while low/mid-level visual axes align with early visual cortex and shallow layers. Ablation and dissociation tests confirm selectivity over candidate morphism families.
AI.conclusion: NVS transforms brain-DNN comparison into a test of jointly preserved transformations relative to an explicit proxy space, revealing structured alignment not captured by existing metrics, and opens pathways to richer proxy spaces and controlled world-side transformations.

title: Mathematical Modeling of Early Embryonic Cell Cycles of Drosophila melanogaster
summary: In the early stages of development, Drosophila melanogaster embryos possess very fast and well-coordinated cell cycles. In the cell cycle, CDK activity is essentially regulated by binding CDK and CycB to form an active complex and by phosphorylating CDK via CDC25 and dephosphorylating it via Wee1. We develop a mathematical model for the embryonic cell cycle which is biochemically sound and which can be rigorously analysed after a model reduction. We show that there exists a region in the parameter space where the model describes oscillations. We then focus on the role of two parameters: the CycB synthesis and the activation coefficient of APC. Our main biological hypothesis is that the first one is responsible for the period lengthening over the first 14 cycles which can be experimentally observed and this hypothesis is supported by numerical simulations of our model: if the CycB synthesis is made time-dependent with a prescribed dynamics, then our simulations show qualitatively a very similar behavior to experimental data reported in the literature.

AI.tldr: A mathematical model of Drosophila embryonic cell cycles shows that time-dependent CycB synthesis explains the observed period lengthening across the first 14 cycles, supported by numerical simulations matching experimental data.
AI.motivation: To understand how Drosophila embryos maintain fast, well-coordinated cell cycles in early development, and to explain the experimentally observed period lengthening over the first 14 cycles using a biochemically sound mathematical model.
AI.method: A mathematical model of the embryonic cell cycle was developed based on CDK-CycB binding, CDC25/Wee1 phosphorylation dynamics, and APC regulation. Model reduction enabled rigorous analysis. Parameter space was explored for oscillatory behavior, with focus on CycB synthesis rate and APC activation coefficient. Time-dependent CycB synthesis was tested via numerical simulations.
AI.result: The model exhibits oscillations in a defined parameter region. Numerical simulations show that making CycB synthesis time-dependent qualitatively reproduces the period lengthening pattern observed across the first 14 embryonic cycles, supporting the hypothesis that CycB synthesis drives this phenomenon.
AI.conclusion: The CycB synthesis rate is a key driver of cell cycle period lengthening during early Drosophila embryogenesis, as validated by the mathematical model's agreement with experimental data.

title: Nationwide EHR-Based Chronic Rhinosinusitis Prediction Using Demographic-Stratified Models
comment: Sicong Chang, Yidan Shen are the co-first authors This paper is already accepted to IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society (EMBC) 2026 conference
summary: Chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) is a common heterogeneous inflammatory disorder that causes substantial morbidity and healthcare costs. CRS is difficult to identify early from routine encounters, as symptom presentations overlap with common conditions such as allergic rhinitis, and heterogeneous phenotypes further obscure risk patterns. Prior predictive studies often rely on single-institutional cohorts , which reduce population-level generalizability. To overcome this, we leveraged nationwide longitudinal EHR data from the \textit{All of Us} Research Program to predict CRS diagnosis using two years of pre-diagnostic history. To address extreme feature sparsity and dimensionality in coded EHR data, we implemented a hybrid feature-selection pipeline that combines prevalence-based statistical screening with model-based importance ranking, compressing approximately 110,000 candidate codes into 100 interpretable features. To capture demographic heterogeneity, we trained demographic stratified models across six adult sex and life-stage subgroups with subgroup-specific hyperparameter tuning. Our framework achieved an overall AUC of 0.8461, improving discrimination by 0.0168 over the best baseline. These results demonstrate that routinely collected EHR data may support population-representative CRS risk stratification and inform earlier triage and referral prioritization in primary care.

AI.tldr: The study used nationwide EHR data from the *All of Us* program to predict chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) diagnosis two years in advance, employing a hybrid feature-selection pipeline and demographic-stratified models, achieving an AUC of 0.8461.
AI.motivation: CRS is a common, heterogeneous disorder that is difficult to identify early due to overlapping symptoms with conditions like allergic rhinitis. Prior predictive studies relied on single-institutional cohorts lacking generalizability.
AI.method: Used nationwide longitudinal EHR data from the *All of Us* program. Implemented a hybrid feature-selection pipeline combining prevalence-based statistical screening with model-based importance ranking, reducing ~110,000 codes to 100 features. Trained demographic-stratified models across six adult sex and life-stage subgroups with subgroup-specific hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: Achieved an overall AUC of 0.8461, improving discrimination by 0.0168 over the best baseline.
AI.conclusion: Routinely collected EHR data may support population-representative CRS risk stratification and inform earlier triage and referral prioritization in primary care.

title: Direct From Darwin: Deriving Advanced Optimizers From Evolutionary First Principles
comment: 38 pages, 5 figures. Submitted to Evolutionary Computation, May 2026. Code available at: https://github.com/danielgrimmer/adam-dls
summary: Evolutionary computation has long promised to deliver both high-performance optimization tools as well as rigorous scientific simulations of Darwinian evolution. However, modern algorithms frequently abandon evolutionary fidelity for physics-inspired heuristics or superficial biological metaphors. This paper derives a suite of advanced gradient-based optimization algorithms directly from evolutionary first principles. We introduce Darwinian Lineage Simulations (DLS) to prove that, in an asexual context, Fisher's and Wright's historically opposed views of evolution are actually formally equivalent. This unification requires carefully partitioning Fisher's deterministically-evolving total population into Wright's randomly-drifting sub-populations. We prove that proper bookkeeping requires introducing a specific kind of structured noise (the DLS noise relation). Crucially, however, any bookkeeping choices which satisfy this relation will result in a faithful simulation of evolution. Using this vast representational freedom, we prove that a broad family of battle-tested optimization algorithms are already perfectly compatible with evolutionary dynamics. These include: Stochastic Gradient Descent, Natural Gradient Descent, and the Damped Newton's method among many others. By simply adding DLS noise (i.e., evolutionarily faithful genetic drift), these algorithms become scientifically valid in silico simulations of Darwinian evolution. Finally, we demonstrate that even the state-of-the-art Adam optimizer can be brought into evolutionary compliance through a minor mathematical surgery.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that gradient-based optimizers (SGD, Adam, etc.) can be made evolutionarily faithful simulations of Darwinian evolution by adding structured noise (DLS noise), unifying Fisher's and Wright's evolutionary views.
AI.motivation: To reconcile the gap between evolutionary computation's promise as both optimization tools and scientific simulations of evolution, by proving that modern gradient-based optimizers can be rigorously grounded in evolutionary first principles.
AI.method: Derives Darwinian Lineage Simulations (DLS) to formally unify Fisher's deterministic and Wright's stochastic views of evolution through partitioning populations and introducing structured DLS noise. Shows that adding this noise transforms existing optimizers into faithful evolutionary simulations.
AI.result: Proves that SGD, Natural Gradient Descent, Damped Newton's method, and even Adam can be made evolutionarily compliant by adding DLS noise (genetic drift). Demonstrates flexibility in bookkeeping choices as long as the DLS noise relation is satisfied.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-based optimization and Darwinian evolution are not fundamentally incompatible; with proper noise injection, mainstream optimizers can serve as valid in silico evolutionary simulations.

title: Resolving the bias-precision paradox with stochastic causal representation learning for personalized medicine
summary: Estimating individualized treatment effects from longitudinal observational data is central to data-driven medicine, yet existing methods face a fundamental limitation: reducing confounding bias often suppresses clinically informative heterogeneity, degrading patient-specific predictions. Here, we identify this tension as a bias-precision paradox in causal representation learning and introduce sampling-based maximum mean discrepancy (sMMD), a stochastic alignment strategy that replaces global adversarial balancing with subset-level matching. We instantiate this approach in a framework for counterfactual outcome prediction with attribution-grounded interpretability. Across two large-scale ICU cohorts (n = 27,783), our framework improves accuracy under distribution shift, reducing error by up to 11.5% and substantially increasing recall in high-risk tasks. Mechanistic analyses show that sMMD selectively preserves clinically decisive variables. In human-AI evaluation, our method outperforms clinicians-in-training and large language models, and improves clinician accuracy by 14.7% while reducing decision time, enabling interpretable, real-time clinical decision support.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces sMMD, a stochastic alignment strategy for causal representation learning that resolves the bias-precision paradox in estimating individualized treatment effects, improving accuracy and interpretability in clinical predictions across two ICU cohorts.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for estimating individualized treatment effects from longitudinal data face a bias-precision paradox: reducing confounding bias suppresses clinically informative heterogeneity, degrading patient-specific predictions.
AI.method: The authors propose sampling-based maximum mean discrepancy (sMMD), a stochastic alignment strategy that replaces global adversarial balancing with subset-level matching, instantiated in a counterfactual outcome prediction framework with attribution-grounded interpretability.
AI.result: Across two ICU cohorts (n=27,783), the method reduces error by up to 11.5%, substantially increases recall in high-risk tasks, and improves clinician accuracy by 14.7% while reducing decision time.
AI.conclusion: sMMD selectively preserves clinically decisive variables, outperforms clinicians-in-training and large language models, and enables interpretable, real-time clinical decision support.

title: BioResearcher: Scenario-Guided Multi-Agent for Translational Medicine
summary:
Translational medicine turns underspecified development goals into evidence synthesis that must combine literature, trials, patents, and quantitative multi-omics analysis while preserving identifiers, uncertainty, and retrievable provenance. General-purpose foundation models and off-the-shelf tool-augmented or multi-agent systems are not built for this: they tend to produce single-shot answers or run open-endedly, and fall short on the auditable, scenario-specific workflows that heterogeneous biomedical sources demand.
  This paper introduces Ingenix BioResearcher, a scenario-guided multi-agent system that maps queries to versioned research playbooks, delegates to specialized subagents over 30+ tools and machine-learning endpoints, mixes structured database access with sandboxed code for genome-scale analyses, and applies claim-level multi-model reconciliation before editorial assembly.
  We evaluate BioResearcher across unit-level capabilities, open-ended biomedical reasoning, and end-to-end clinical discovery. It leads evaluated baselines on 109 single-step tests (83.49% pass rate; 0.892 average score), achieves strong biomedical benchmark performance (89.33% on BixBench-Verified-50 and the top 0.758 mean score on BaisBench Scientific Discovery), and leads on a 30-query clinical end-to-end benchmark with the highest positive hit rate (74.7% $\pm$ 3.3%) and negative clear rate (96.8% $\pm$ 0.2%). These results show broad, competitive performance across unit-level, open-ended, and end-to-end clinical evaluations.

AI.tldr: Ingenix BioResearcher is a scenario-guided multi-agent system for translational medicine that maps queries to versioned research playbooks, uses specialized agents across 30+ tools, and achieves strong benchmark results including 83.49% pass rate on single-step tests and 74.7% positive hit rate on clinical end-to-end queries.
AI.motivation: Translational medicine requires evidence synthesis combining literature, trials, patents, and multi-omics data while preserving identifiers, uncertainty, and retrievable provenance. Existing foundation models and multi-agent systems aren't built for this—they produce single-shot answers or run open-endedly and lack auditable, scenario-specific workflows for heterogeneous biomedical sources.
AI.method: Ingenix BioResearcher is a scenario-guided multi-agent system that: (1) maps queries to versioned research playbooks, (2) delegates to specialized subagents over 30+ tools and ML endpoints, (3) mixes structured database access with sandboxed code for genome-scale analyses, and (4) applies claim-level multi-model reconciliation before editorial assembly.
AI.result: Leads baselines on 109 single-step tests (83.49% pass rate; 0.892 avg score), achieves 89.33% on BixBench-Verified-50, top 0.758 mean score on BaisBench Scientific Discovery, and leads on a 30-query clinical benchmark with highest positive hit rate (74.7% ± 3.3%) and negative clear rate (96.8% ± 0.2%).
AI.conclusion: Ingenix BioResearcher demonstrates broad, competitive performance across unit-level, open-ended, and end-to-end clinical evaluations, suggesting it is well-suited for auditable, scenario-specific translational medicine workflows.

title: A Versatile AI Agent for Rare Disease Diagnosis and Risk Gene Prioritization
summary: Accurate and timely diagnosis is essential for effective treatment, particularly in the context of rare diseases. However, current diagnostic workflows often lead to prolonged assessment times and low accuracy. To address these limitations, we introduce Hygieia, a multi-modal AI agent system designed to support precision disease diagnosis by integrating diverse data sources, including phenotypic features, genetic profiles, and clinical records. Hygieia features a router-based and knowledge-enhanced framework that mitigates hallucination and tailors diagnostic strategies to different disease categories. Notably, it prioritizes risk-related genomic factors for rare diseases and provides confidence scores to assist clinical decision-making. We conducted a comprehensive evaluation demonstrating that Hygieia achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple diagnostic benchmarks. In collaboration with clinical experts from Yale School of Medicine and Duke-NUS Medical School, we further validated its practical utility by showing (1) Hygieia's superior diagnostic performance compared to physicians with an improvement from 12%-60% and (2) its effectiveness in assisting clinicians with medical records for handling real-world cases. Our findings indicate that Hygieia not only enhances diagnostic accuracy and interpretability but also significantly reduces clinician workload, highlighting its potential as a valuable tool in clinical decision support systems.

AI.tldr: Hygieia is a multi-modal AI agent system that integrates phenotypic, genetic, and clinical data to improve rare disease diagnosis, achieving 12-60% better accuracy than physicians and reducing clinician workload.
AI.motivation: Current diagnostic workflows for rare diseases suffer from prolonged assessment times and low accuracy, highlighting the need for more effective diagnostic tools.
AI.method: Hygieia uses a router-based, knowledge-enhanced framework with multi-modal data integration (phenotypic, genetic, clinical features), hallucination mitigation, risk-prioritized genomic analysis, and confidence scoring.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks; improves diagnostic accuracy by 12-60% over physicians; validated with clinical experts from Yale and Duke-NUS.
AI.conclusion: Hygieia enhances diagnostic accuracy and interpretability while significantly reducing clinician workload, demonstrating strong potential as a clinical decision support tool for rare diseases.

title: A $μ$-distance for semidirected orchard phylogenetic networks
summary: In evolutionary biology, phylogenetic networks are now widely used to represent the historical relationships between species and population, when this history includes reticulation events such as hybridization, gene flow and admixture between populations. Semidirected phylogenetic networks are appropriate models when the direction of some edges and the root position are not identifiable from data. Comparing semidirected networks is important in many applications. For rooted and directed networks, a $μ$-representation was originally introduced to distinguish tree-child networks, and has since been extended in two different directions: to the larger class of orchard directed networks by adding an extra component that counts paths to reticulations; and to semidirected networks, through an edge-based variant. However, the latter does not provide a distance between semidirected and orchard networks. We introduce here a new edge-based $μ$-representation capable of distinguishing distinct orchard binary semidirected networks. For this class, we provide a reconstruction algorithm and therefore obtain a true distance that is computable in polynomial time.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new edge-based μ-representation that can distinguish orchard binary semidirected phylogenetic networks, providing a polynomial-time computable distance and reconstruction algorithm for this class.
AI.motivation: Semidirected phylogenetic networks are important models when edge direction and root position are not identifiable from data, but existing μ-representations either work only for smaller network classes (tree-child) or fail to provide a distance for semidirected orchard networks.
AI.method: The authors propose a new edge-based μ-representation that extends previous approaches by incorporating additional structural information to distinguish orchard binary semidirected networks, along with a reconstruction algorithm.
AI.result: The new representation can distinguish distinct orchard binary semidirected networks, and enables a polynomial-time computable distance metric.
AI.conclusion: This work fills a gap in phylogenetic network comparison by providing the first distance measure for semidirected orchard networks that is both discriminative and efficiently computable.

title: Activation in Vesicle-Mediated Signaling Shaped by Batch Arrival Statistics
summary: Vesicle-mediated secretion of ions or molecules is a central mechanism of cellular communication, for example in processes such as neurotransmission or hormone release. These events are inherently stochastic: vesicle fusions lead to bursts of variable sizes, releasing discrete packets of transmitters that are subsequently cleared or degraded. The dynamics break time-reversal symmetry due to the interplay of spontaneous bursts and continuous degradation. Using generating functions and a recursion relation, we derive an exact solution for the full time-dependent probability distribution of a general batch arrival-degradation model. This framework also enables a full analysis of first-passage times to a concentration threshold representing downstream activation. We show that activation kinetics are not determined by mean dynamics alone, but depend sensitively on the temporal statistics of arrival events, batch-size variability, and degradation. In particular, different arrival processes with identical mean rates can lead to qualitatively distinct first-passage behavior, reflecting the role of time-asymmetric fluctuations. We also discuss extensions incorporating vesicle depletion. Our results provide a transparent link between stochastic release dynamics and activation timing in vesicle-mediated signaling.

AI.tldr: This paper derives an exact solution for the full time-dependent probability distribution of a batch arrival-degradation model describing stochastic vesicle-mediated secretion, and shows that activation kinetics depend sensitively on temporal statistics beyond mean dynamics.
AI.motivation: To understand how stochastic vesicle fusion events and degradation dynamics determine activation timing in cellular signaling processes like neurotransmission and hormone release.
AI.method: Using generating functions and recursion relations to analytically solve the batch arrival-degradation model, and analyzing first-passage times to concentration thresholds.
AI.result: Activation kinetics are not determined by mean dynamics alone but depend sensitively on temporal statistics of arrival events, batch-size variability, and degradation; different arrival processes with identical mean rates can yield qualitatively distinct first-passage behaviors.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a transparent link between stochastic release dynamics and activation timing in vesicle-mediated signaling, with extensions to vesicle depletion.

title: Feature Dimensionality Outweighs Model Complexity in Breast Cancer Subtype Classification Using TCGA-BRCA Gene Expression Data
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Independent research study using TCGA-BRCA RNA-seq data
summary:
Accurate classification of breast cancer subtypes from gene expression data is critical for diagnosis and treatment selection. However, such datasets are characterized by high dimensionality and limited sample size, posing challenges for machine learning models.
  In this study, we evaluate the impact of model complexity and feature selection on subtype classification performance using TCGA-BRCA gene expression data. Logistic regression, random forest, and support vector machine (SVM) models were trained using varying numbers of highly variable genes (50 to 20,518). Performance was evaluated using stratified 5-fold cross-validation and assessed with accuracy and macro F1 score. While all models achieved high accuracy, macro F1 analysis revealed substantial differences in subtype-level performance. Logistic regression demonstrated the most stable and balanced performance across subtypes, including improved detection of rare classes. Random forest underperformed on minority subtypes despite strong overall accuracy, while SVM showed sensitivity to feature dimensionality. These findings highlight the importance of model simplicity, evaluation metrics, and feature selection in high-dimensional biological classification tasks.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates logistic regression, random forest, and SVM for breast cancer subtype classification using TCGA-BRCA gene expression data, finding that logistic regression provides the most balanced performance across subtypes, especially for rare classes, while random forest underperforms on minority subtypes and SVM is sensitive to feature dimensionality.
AI.motivation: Accurate classification of breast cancer subtypes from gene expression data is critical for diagnosis and treatment, but high dimensionality and limited sample size pose challenges for machine learning models.
AI.method: Logistic regression, random forest, and SVM were trained on TCGA-BRCA gene expression data using varying numbers of highly variable genes (50 to 20,518). Performance was evaluated using stratified 5-fold cross-validation with accuracy and macro F1 score.
AI.result: All models achieved high accuracy, but macro F1 revealed differences: logistic regression showed the most stable and balanced performance across subtypes; random forest underperformed on minority subtypes; SVM was sensitive to feature dimensionality.
AI.conclusion: Model simplicity, appropriate evaluation metrics, and feature selection are critical in high-dimensional biological classification tasks.
